risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The report describes a prospective cohort/nested validation study of participants from the Swiss HIV Cohort Study, but it does not describe any allocation or randomization procedure. There is no statement that participants were randomized, randomly allocated, or assigned by any random method, and no evidence of a non-random method either. Because random sequence generation is not reported, the risk of bias for this domain cannot be judged and remains unclear.

Summary: The report describes cohort enrollment from the Swiss HIV Cohort Study and gives inclusion criteria, but it does not state any method used to conceal upcoming assignments from the person enrolling participants. There is no mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, sequentially numbered identical containers, or any other concealment mechanism. The text is about diagnostic testing and follow-up rather than randomized group assignment, so allocation concealment cannot be judged from the available information.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: This was a diagnostic/prognostic cohort study using transient elastography and blood markers, with outcomes based on objective laboratory/imaging measures and histopathology. The paper explicitly states that the pathologist was blinded to non-invasive test results and clinical course, and the non-invasive tests themselves are objective measurements, making it unlikely that participants/personnel knowledge of allocation would influence the outcomes. Comment: Although the study does not describe participant/personnel blinding to a randomized intervention, performance bias is not plausibly important for the main outcomes reported here.

Quote: "All histopathologic assessments were performed centrally by an experienced pathologist (WJ) who was not aware of the results of the non-invasive liver fibrosis tests and the clinical course of the patients." Comment: The outcome assessor for the primary outcome (liver biopsy histopathology) was an independent pathologist explicitly blinded to the test results and clinical course. Histopathological staging is a judgement-based outcome, so assessor blinding is relevant, and the explicit blinding described here supports low risk of detection bias for this outcome. The non-invasive blood tests were also objective laboratory-derived measures, so lack of assessor blinding would be unlikely to influence their measurement.

Quote: "87 (83%) of 105 enrolled participants had a complete follow up of 3 years. Eight patients died during the follow-up period... Nine patients dropped out due to several reasons (e.g. moving away, non-adherence). One patient was excluded from the analysis because of liver transplantation..." Comment: Although there was some attrition in the 3-year follow-up, the paper reports the reasons and indicates that 87/105 (83%) completed follow-up, which is within a range that is generally acceptable. The reasons given (death, moving away, non-adherence, transplantation) are described and do not clearly suggest differential, outcome-related dropout across groups; the study also states that all participants with complete 3-year follow-up were analysed.
